default_annotator.report_text:
a suspicious email that targeted a government official from Jordan's foreign ministry. The email contained a malicious Excel document that drops a new backdoor named Saitama.

The malicious email was sent to the victim via a Microsoft Outlook account with the subject "Confirmation Receive Document" with an Excel file called "Confirmation Receive Document.xls". The sender pretends to be a person from the Government of Jordan by using its coat of arms as a signature.

The Excel attachment contains a macro that performs malicious activities.

The document has an image that tries to convince the victim to enable a macro.

After enabling the macro, the image is replaced with the Jordan government's coat of arms.

Hides the current sheet and shows the new sheet that contains the coat of arms image.

Calls the "eNotif" function which is used to send a notification of each steps of macro execution to its server using the DNS protocol.

Creates a TaskService object and Gets the task folder that contains the list of the current tasks.

it uses the following WMI query to get the IP address of the request.

Checks if there is a mouse connected to PC.

Reads the content of the UserForm1.label1, UserForm2.label1 and UserForm3.label1 that are in base64 format, decodes them and finally writes them into the created files in the previous step.

Checks the existence of the Update.exe file.

it writes it using a technique that loads a DotNet assembly directly using mscorlib and Assembly.Load by manually accessing the VTable of the IUnknown.

Defines a xml schema for a scheduled task and registers it using the RegisterTask function. The name of the scheduled task is MicrosoftUpdate and is used to make update.exe persistent.

Saitama backdoor abuses the DNS protocol for its command and control communications.

the actor cleverly uses techniques such as compression.

This state fetches the C&C server, expecting to receive a command from the attackers. These servers are generated by using the PRNG algorithm that involves transformations like the Mersenne Twister.

As domain names are used to exfiltrate unknown amounts of data, attackers had to split this data in different buffers. Every buffer is then sent through a different DNS request.

scheduled task that would launch the executable every X minutes.

anti sandboxing technique (checking if there is a mouse connected to the PC or not).
